title: Contract exception pathway: MSA/DPA, pricing approval & privileged access sync
summary: Quick sync on an MSA/DPA contract exception route for privileged access, clarification on KMS/HSM clauses, and an ask to accelerate pricing approval for a pilot. AE + SE led; legal and procurement present. Agreed to provide redlines, security pack, and pilot pricing sheet by EOD next week.
topics: DPA data residency and audit log obligations
Next steps for POC onboarding and SSO/KMS integration
next_steps: Redwood to send annotated MSA + DPA with proposed exception language — Ari — by 2025-01-16
Share security pack (SOC2 report, pen test summary, KMS integration doc) — Samira — by 2025-01-16
Sentinel to confirm internal approvers and final budgetary sign-off date — Priya — by 2025-01-14
Schedule technical onboarding call for SSO/KMS mapping — Jon/Sentinal Evan — week of 2025-01-20
action_items: Ari Chen | Send annotated MSA + DPA with exception path | 2025-01-16
Samira Patel | Provide security pack & HSM/KMS integration notes | 2025-01-16
Priya Rao | Share procurement approval owners and timeline | 2025-01-14
Evan Liu | Provide service account matrix and firewall egress list | 2025-01-15
transcript:
Meeting header: 2025-01-09 16:00 PST | Duration: ~46 minutes | Attendees listed above

[S00:00] Ari (Redwood AE): Hey everyone, thanks for jumping on. I think we've got a packed agenda — quick intro then go into the exception path, pricing for the pilot, and technical privileged access concerns. Mark, Nadia, Priya — thanks for making time.

[S00:18] Mark (Sentinel CISO): Thanks Ari. Quick background — we're evaluating Redwood for a clinical notes summarization pilot. Primary blockers for procurement are the MSA exceptions, DPA tweaks around residency, and the need to limit privileged access.

[S00:35] Priya (Procurement): And to add, finance needs a firm pilot price and a clean path to sign-off. We have to route anything with exceptions through our legal gate.

[S00:48] Ari: Totally. Samira, do you want to walk through the typical exception flow and what we've seen approved?

[S00:55] Samira (Redwood SE): Yeah sure. So ordinarily we ship the standard MSA/DPA. For cases like Sentinel where there's a request for privileged access or HSM-level keys, we usually go through a documented exceptions pathway: redline the MSA, capture a mitigation plan, and get a sponsor from the business unit to sign off. Usually two legal reviews and a SOC2 attestation check. Apologies, I said two legal reviews — sometimes it's one if counsel accepts our compensating controls.

[S01:27] Nadia (Sentinel Legal): What's in your compensating controls typically? We need clear language on audit logs, key custody, and retention. We also want to understand breach notification timelines.

[S01:40] Samira: Right — we provide audit log retention options, per-tenant audit exports, SSO/SAML for access controls, and we support customer-managed keys via KMS/HSM integrations in Private deployments or VPC options. For hosted API we can do envelope encryption and a bring-your-own-key (BYOK) in certain tiers. Note: that BYOK path has vendor-side audit hooks.

[S02:05] Mark: When you say BYOK for hosted, are keys in HW-backed HSMs? Or is it KMS software keys?

[S02:13] Samira: For hosted BYOK we partner with cloud KMS — keys are stored in customer-managed cloud KMS; we do an HSM-backed option if you're on a Dedicated or Private plan with HSM support. There's some nuance — I might have said HSM-backed by default earlier, sorry.

[S02:30] Tom (Finance): Pricing — can we get the pilot cost outline? We need a three-month capped cost not to exceed X. Also curious about overage handling.

[S02:42] Ari: We've put together a pilot pricing template. For the size you described — about 10k tokens/day peak — we're proposing a reserved mini-pool + burst on Hosted. We can cap spend and put a spend alert. I'll send a detailed sheet. The key ask: if you need a firm three-month fixed fee, that requires a short reserved commitment.

[S03:05] Priya: If a reserved fee is required that's fine, but procurement needs to know approval route if legal asks for an exception. Do you have a playbook?

[S03:17] Ari: We do. The playbook is: 1) Redwood provides annotated MSA/DPA with exception language; 2) customer raises internal risk acceptance with sponsor + legal; 3) for privileged access we add compensating controls and a privileged access SOP; 4) final sign-off — procurement approves. The whole loop usually takes 2–4 weeks depending on cycles.

[S03:46] Nadia: We need explicit SLA language around data deletion and audit trails in the DPA. Also, the DPA needs to specify data residency — we will not accept cross-border processing for PHI without additional controls.

[S03:57] Samira: Understood. For residency we can pin region and provide dedicated capacity or Private deployment. For hosted, we can restrict processing to region-limited endpoints and document the flow. There are occasional transcription errors here — we call out KV cache as K-V cash in some transcripts, ha.

[S04:20] Evan (Platform Eng): Quick technical note — how do you handle KV cache invalidation and are any user data persisted to disk on the inference nodes? That's a big one for us.

[S04:30] Samira: Good question. The KV cache is in-memory by default and evicted per TTL on the node; we persist checkpoints for model state onto ephemeral encrypted disk that is rotated. No long-term storage of raw inputs unless you enable logging. If you need zero-persistence we can disable request logging and enable prefix caching only in memory. Sorry, that sounded a bit dense — TL;DR: no persistent storage of inputs by default in our hosted runtime.

[S04:58] Mark: How are audit logs delivered? S3 export or syslog push?

[S05:05] Samira: S3 export, SIEM integration via webhook/syslog, or direct API pulls. We can do a push to your Splunk endpoint.

[S05:16] Nadia: On breach notification — our policy requires 48-hour notification. Can Redwood commit to that?

[S05:23] Ari: Our standard is 72 hours. We can escalate with executive contact, but changing to 48 is an exception. We can propose a mitigated exception: 72 hours with near-real-time detection hooks and a joint runbook — would that be acceptable?

[S05:40] Nadia: Possibly, but procurement will flag that. We'll capture it as a risk.

[S05:45] Priya: Quick question on indemnity caps — we may need a carveout for gross negligence/intentional misconduct. We typically seek higher buckets for PHI.

[S05:56] Ari: We can discuss limited carveouts. Our legal team rarely accepts uncapped liabilities — we can propose a higher cap for gross negligence but need counsel to review. I'll send suggested language in the annotated MSA.

[S06:10] Tom: On billing, can we get a preview of invoice line items? We need to align with our finance system.

[S06:16] Ari: Yes — pilot fee line, reserved capacity line, overage line, and a one-time onboarding professional services line if you want SE-hours. We'll include a simplified invoice sample.

[S06:27] Evan: For SSO, we use SAML with Okta. What attributes do you require and how is access provisioned for privileged accounts?

[S06:36] Samira: We support SAML/SSO with SCIM provisioning for user roles. For privileged accounts we recommend a break-glass procedure, MFA enforced, and short-lived elevated sessions. We'll share our role matrix.

[S06:50] Mark: For privileged access we need a full list of vendor-side roles with their exact capabilities and an audit trail of actions.

[S06:58] Samira: We have that — will include in the security pack. It includes API-level roles, console roles, and the mapping to SCIM attributes.

[S07:10] Ari: To make progress today, we propose two parallel tracks: legal/procurement work the MSA/DPA exceptions, and the SE team coordinates a technical onboarding for SSO/KMS mapping. Does that work?

[S07:22] Priya: Yes. Also, can Redwood provide a written exception acceptance template that our risk team can sign? It speeds things up.

[S07:30] Ari: We can provide a templated exception acceptance — short form — that summarizes the compensating controls and the acceptance signatures. We'll include it in the redline packet.

[S07:40] Nadia: One more legal nit — data deletion requests. We need a 7-day window for purging customer data after contract termination. Is that feasible?

[S07:50] Samira: We can commit to 7 days for ephemeral inputs and log deletion; backups may be subject to a longer retention (but we can scrub pointers). We'll note that with precise language in the DPA proposal.

[S08:05] Tom: Overage caps — we need to ensure burst doesn't blow the budget. Can you set hard caps?

[S08:12] Ari: Yes, we can implement hard caps per account and send alerts. If you prefer not to allow burst, we can restrict bursts entirely.

[S08:20] Evan: For the POC, what telemetry will you provide? Token-level billing, latency SLOs, error rates?

[S08:27] Samira: We'll provide per-route token usage, latency histograms, error traces, and model versioning info. Also, we'll include per-token cost estimations so you can map to product metrics.

[S08:40] Mark: Any references for customers who have done similar privileged-access arrangements?

[S08:45] Ari: We can share redacted case studies — I'll pull a short note. VendorX has a paper but it's not apples-to-apples.

[S09:00] Nadia: Workflow: once we get your annotated MSA, what's the expected internal review cycle? We need an ETA.

[S09:08] Ari: If you get the annotated docs by Friday, you could do internal review in 5–10 business days if your legal is quick. Otherwise 2–4 weeks. We'll flag urgent items inline to accelerate.

[S09:22] Priya: Who is our escalation if procurement hits a blocker?

[S09:26] Jon (CSM): I'm the designated CSM — Jon Park. I can coordinate with Ari and Samira and loop in product/engineering if any technical guardrail questions surface.

[S09:35] Priya: Good. Please include Jon in the exception packet.

[S09:40] Ari: Will do. Quick recap and then we'll go line-by-line for questions — any immediate redlines you foresee, Nadia?

[S09:48] Nadia: Breach timeline, data residency, indemnity cap carveouts for PHI, and privileged access auditability are the main ones. We'll flag any others after internal review.

[S10:00] Ari: Great. We'll prioritize those in the annotated MSA.

[S10:05] -- brief pause while Ari shares link to the pilot pricing sheet --

[S10:20] Ari: I just dropped the pilot pricing doc in the chat — it's a one-page summary and a fuller spreadsheet. Tom, this should give you the capped three-month numbers.

[S10:32] Tom: Got it. Looks reasonable. One question on the PS hours line — can those be shifted to a second PO? We prefer that for accounting reasons.

[S10:40] Ari: Yes, PS can be billed separately or included; we'll note both options.

[S10:45] Evan: Back to the KMS question — if we run a Private deployment on our VPC, do we still need Redwood-managed keys?

[S10:52] Samira: For VPC/private deployments you can supply your own HSM/KMS and Redwood will never hold keys. In that mode, we operate with customer-managed keys and a control plane that integrates via your KMS endpoints. There are extra ops costs for that path.

[S11:07] Mark: Ok that's important. We'll likely prefer Private for PHI unless cost/ops prohibit it.

[S11:13] Ari: Understood. We'll include cost delta between hosted with BYOK and Private with full customer KMS.

[S11:20] Nadia: On the DPA, please make the retention table explicit — what elements are retained, duration, and purge behavior.

[S11:28] Samira: We'll add a retention annex with a retention matrix for inputs, logs, model checkpoints, and telemetry.

[S11:36] Priya: One last procurement question — what's the SLA credit model? If latency issues cause a miss, how are credits handled and are they escrowed?

[S11:46] Ari: Standard SLA credits are usage-based credits applied to invoices. For enterprise/Dedicated we can negotiate a financial credit model in the contract. We don't currently escrow credits.

[S11:58] Nadia: Might be a blocker but we'll flag internally.

[S12:05] Ari: OK, time check — we're at about 46 minutes — let's do a quick recap of agreed owner actions.

[S12:12] Ari: Action recap: 1) Ari send annotated MSA/DPA and templated exception acceptance; 2) Samira send security pack and role matrix; 3) Jon coordinate onboarding call; 4) Priya provide approver list and timeline; 5) Evan provide firewall/egress list. All due next week.

[S12:30] Priya: Sounds good. We'll aim to have internal approvers named by Wednesday.

[S12:35] Tom: Finance will review the pilot pricing sheet and confirm whether PS can be on a separate PO.

[S12:40] Mark: Thanks — please highlight any 48-hour breach notification ask as an exception in the packet.

[S12:48] Ari: Will do. Thanks everyone, we'll send the packet and follow up with calendar invites for the onboarding.

[S12:55] Nadia: Thanks all. Bye.

[End of transcript]

Notes: Transcript contains occasional auto-caption errors (e.g., "KV cache" transcribed as "K-V cash"). Speakers labeled with role names where provided. The technical details were intentionally high-level to avoid exposing exact production runtime internals in a recorded transcript.
